narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put together a competitive profile that includes a strong LSAT score of 40—above the typical 36.7 average—and an undergraduate GPA of 3.10, which is close to the standard 3.21. However, the university's admission model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your risk of failure at 40.9%, tipping the decision toward rejection to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This prediction wasn't driven by your standout LSAT or background but hinged on three key factors from your academic record, ranked by their influence on the outcome. Your LSAT score was the most powerful positive force, strongly pushing the model toward expecting you to pass, as it signals exceptional preparation compared to peers. In contrast, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2—well below the average 5.53—had the next biggest impact, nudging the prediction toward failure by highlighting a dip in performance relative to classmates. Similarly, your Year 1 decile of 1, far under the typical 5.71, reinforced this concern, suggesting early struggles that the model sees as a red flag for bar success. While your full-time status aligns with most applicants and didn't sway things much, these decile rankings stood out as weaker than averages, outweighing your strengths in the model's eyes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your later-year rankings, as even small improvements there could flip future predictions. You might also gather letters from professors attesting to your growth since Year 1, or target schools with holistic reviews that weigh your LSAT edge more heavily. With your profile's potential, targeted steps like these could position you strongly for the next cycle.
